concept: **sugar, sugar:** Players interact with the game by drawing lines on the screen with a finger swipe, creating physical barriers that redirect falling sugar particles. The objective for each of the 125 levels is to guide a specific volume of sugar from a fixed source into one or more target cups, which have individual capacity requirements. As players progress, new puzzle elements are introduced, such as color filters that change sugar properties, gravitational switches that alter the direction of sugar flow, and teleports that instantly relocate particles, requiring strategic line placement. Difficulty scales by increasing the number of cups, the complexity of the environment, and the intricate interactions between these new mechanics. A level is completed when all cups receive their required sugar volume, and players advance to the next challenge.
name: sugar, sugar

concept: **Playdead's LIMBO:** Players control a boy in a 2D side-scrolling world, navigating through a series of environmental puzzles and platforming challenges. Movement is controlled by swiping left or right on the left side of the screen, while swiping up on the left side initiates a jump. To interact with objects like crates, levers, or ropes, players tap and hold on the right side of the screen, allowing for pushing, pulling, or climbing actions. The game features a linear progression through distinct, interconnected areas, with difficulty scaling through increasingly complex physics-based puzzles and deadlier environmental hazards. Upon death, which is frequent and often a learning mechanic, the player instantly respawns at the last checkpoint, encouraging trial-and-error to advance through the narrative.
name: Playdead's LIMBO

concept: **Slayin:** Players select one of six unique hero classes and control them in a single-screen arena by tapping the left side of the screen to move left or the right side to move right, with the hero automatically attacking enemies upon weapon collision. Enemies continuously spawn from the screen's edges at increasing rates and speeds, each with distinct movement patterns and health values, requiring precise positioning to defeat. Defeating enemies awards experience points for leveling up, which grants stat increases and new abilities, alongside coins used to purchase equipment upgrades like Sword +1 or Armor +2 from a periodically appearing shopkeeper. The game features endless progression where difficulty scales through escalating enemy density and the introduction of new monster types and boss encounters every few minutes. A run concludes instantly when the hero's health points reach zero, resulting in permanent death and the objective of achieving the highest score based on monsters slain and time survived.
name: Slayin

concept: **Shadow Fight 2 Special Edition:** Players engage in 1v1 martial arts combat against AI opponents using a virtual D-pad on the left for movement (forward, backward, crouch, jump) and two virtual buttons on the right for punch and kick. Combining D-pad inputs with attack buttons executes a diverse arsenal of dozens of special moves and combos, each with specific damage and animation properties. Combat involves depleting the opponent's health bar across a best-of-three rounds format, with players earning coins and gems from victories to purchase and upgrade weapons, armor, and magic spells, which directly impact attack power, defense, and speed stats. The game features a linear story progression across 7 distinct provinces, with each province introducing progressively stronger enemy types and culminating in a challenging boss fight that gates further advancement. Difficulty scales as enemy AI becomes more aggressive and their stats increase, necessitating strategic gear upgrades and mastery of new combat techniques to complete the story and defeat Titan.
name: Shadow Fight 2 Special Edition

concept: **Terraforming Mars:** Players lead a corporation to terraform Mars over a series of generations, primarily by playing project cards from their hand that cost MegaCredits (MC) and have effects on global parameters or player resources. Interaction involves tapping cards to select them, then tapping on the game board to place tiles like cities or forests, or tapping on global trackers to increase Temperature, Oxygen, or create Oceans. Each generation cycles through a research phase for drawing new cards, an action phase for playing cards and activating standard projects, and a production phase for resource generation based on current ratings. Players earn Terraform Rating (TR) points by increasing global parameters, which also boosts their income, and accumulate Victory Points (VP) from TR, played cards, and achieving specific milestones like building 3 cities. The game concludes when all global parameters (Temperature to +8°C, Oxygen to 14%, 9 Oceans) reach their target values, and the player with the highest total Victory Points wins.
name: Terraforming Mars

concept: **SteamWorld Quest:** Players control a party of heroes in turn-based combat, managing a hand of 6-8 "punch-cards" drawn from a customizable deck of over 100 unique cards. During their turn, players tap to select up to 3 cards to play, consuming "Steam" energy points which regenerate by 1-2 points per turn, to attack enemies, defend, or apply status effects with specific numerical values (e.g., 15 physical damage, restore 10 HP). Progression involves earning experience points from victorious battles to level up heroes, increasing their base HP and attack, while collected gold allows for purchasing new cards or upgrading existing ones to enhance their effects (e.g., +5 damage, -1 Steam cost). Combat concludes when all enemy health pools are depleted, or all player heroes are defeated, with the primary objective being to advance through story chapters and unlock new heroes and powerful card combinations.
name: SteamWorld Quest

concept: **The Game of Life:** Players engage in a digital board game experience, taking turns to spin a numerical spinner (1-10 spaces) by tapping it, which dictates their movement along a linear path on a 3D board. Landing on specific spaces prompts players to make life choices (e.g., "Go to College" or "Start Career"), encounter events, or participate in simple tap-based minigames where success grants bonus in-game currency. The core loop involves accumulating wealth and "Life Points" through strategic choices and successful minigames, with progression defined by advancing through life stages towards the "Retirement" space. The game concludes when all players reach retirement, or a player goes bankrupt and is eliminated, with the winner being the player who amassed the most total wealth (money plus assets). Multiplayer modes support up to three local players on one device or online matchmaking against other players, with a "Fast Mode" offering alternative win conditions like being the first to reach $1,000,000.
name: The Game of Life

concept: **Doodle God™:** Players begin with four fundamental elements: Air, Earth, Fire, and Water, displayed in categorized lists on the user interface. The primary mechanic involves dragging one element from its category and dropping it onto another element to attempt a combination, with successful pairings creating a new, unique element (e.g., Water + Earth = Mud) that is then added to the player's available inventory. Progression is centered on discovering over 300 distinct elements, each expanding the player's creative possibilities and unlocking new categories for further experimentation. "Planet Mode" visually tracks the player's progress by animating discovered elements onto a growing planet, while "Mission" and "Puzzle" modes present specific creation objectives, such as crafting a "Locomotive" from a predefined set of starting elements. The game's core loop is a continuous cycle of combining existing elements to discover new ones, with the ultimate goal being to unlock the entire universe of available creations.
name: Doodle God™

concept: **Hitman Sniper:** Players assume the role of Agent 47, positioned at a fixed sniper nest overlooking a multi-story compound, using tap-and-drag gestures to aim their sniper scope, pinching to zoom, and tapping to fire. The core mechanic involves identifying and eliminating primary targets and secondary objectives within a mission's time limit (e.g., 5 minutes), utilizing environmental interactions like shooting explosive barrels or dropping objects to create silent kills. Successful eliminations and stealthy takedowns contribute to a score multiplier, while detection by guards or failure to eliminate primary targets results in immediate mission failure. Progression is driven by earning cash and weapon parts from completed missions, which are used to unlock and upgrade 17 unique sniper rifles, each possessing distinct stats for damage, zoom, and stability. An additional "Death Valley" mode challenges players with wave-based zombie survival, emphasizing precision and speed against increasing enemy numbers.
name: ヒットマン スナイパー (Hitman Sniper)

concept: **MONOPOLY:** Players participate in a digital board game by taking turns tapping to roll two six-sided dice, moving their chosen token around the iconic Monopoly board. The core mechanic involves acquiring properties by landing on unowned spaces and purchasing them for a fixed price, or participating in an auction if the property is declined. Landing on an opponent's property requires paying rent, which scales significantly based on property development, allowing up to four houses and then one hotel per property. Progression is session-based, focused on strategically bankrupting all other players through rent collection and property monopolies, while "Chance" and "Community Chest" cards introduce random financial or movement events. The game concludes when only one player remains solvent, declared the winner, with multiplayer options including local pass-and-play on a single device or online matchmaking against up to three other players.
name: モノポリー (MONOPOLY)

concept: **Danganronpa 2: Goodbye Despair:** Players progress through a story-driven murder mystery, alternating between investigation and class trial phases across a tropical island setting. During investigations, players navigate 3D environments using virtual joystick or tap-to-move controls, tapping on objects and characters to gather 5-10 pieces of evidence and testimony per chapter. Class trials involve presenting collected evidence to "shoot down" contradictory statements from other characters, often through minigames where players must tap or swipe to select the correct evidence within a 5-10 second time limit per statement. Successful completion of a trial advances the narrative to the next chapter, while failing to identify the killer or making too many incorrect deductions (e.g., 3 incorrect choices) results in a game over, requiring a retry from a checkpoint. Progression unlocks new story segments and adds content to the Intimacy, Character, and Ultimate Galleries, with minigame difficulty scaling dynamically based on player performance or chapter progression. The UI features optimized touch controls for 3D map movement and a "map jumping" function for instant travel between 5-8 key locations within an area.
name: Danganronpa 2: Goodbye Despair

concept: **Kniffel® Klassik:** Kniffel is a turn-based digital dice game where 1-8 players compete to achieve the highest score by rolling five six-sided dice. On each turn, a player gets up to three rolls: they tap a "Roll" button to initiate the first roll, then tap individual dice on the screen to "hold" them before tapping "Roll" again for subsequent re-rolls of the unheld dice. After the third roll, or earlier if satisfied, the player must select one of 13 available scoring categories (e.g., "Three of a Kind," "Full House," "Kniffel/Yahtzee") from a digital score sheet to record their points, with each category usable only once per game. The game progresses through rounds until all 13 scoring categories are filled by every player, at which point the player with the highest cumulative score is declared the winner. Difficulty for AI opponents can be selected from 3 levels (e.g., Easy, Medium, Hard), influencing their strategic scoring decisions, while local multiplayer supports up to 8 players sharing a single device. The UI features clear dice display, tap-to-hold functionality, and a dynamic score sheet, with optional motion sensor input allowing players to shake their device to simulate a dice roll.
name: Kniffel® Klassik

concept: **Les Aventuriers du Rail®:** Ticket to Ride is a turn-based digital board game where 2-5 players compete to build railway routes across a map, connecting cities to complete destination tickets and earn points. Players begin with 45 train cars, 4 train cards, and 3 destination tickets (keeping at least 2), then take one of three actions per turn: drawing two train cards (from five face-up cards or blind from the deck), drawing three new destination tickets (keeping at least one), or claiming a route by playing a set of matching colored train cards equal to the route's length. Claimed routes are visually represented by placing the player's train cars on the digital board, earning immediate points based on the route's length, while destination tickets award bonus points if completed or penalize if uncompleted at game end. The game concludes when any player has two or fewer train cars remaining, triggering a final round for all players, after which bonus points are awarded for the longest continuous railway and the player with the highest total score wins. Controls involve tapping to draw cards or tickets, and dragging-and-dropping train cards onto desired routes for claiming, with AI opponents offering 3-5 difficulty levels and online multiplayer supporting both real-time and asynchronous play.
name: Les Aventuriers du Rail®

concept: **殺戮の天使 (Angels of Death):** Angels of Death is a story-driven horror adventure game where players control Rachel, a 13-year-old girl, navigating a multi-floor, sealed building to escape with the help of a serial killer, Zack. Players explore 2D environments using tap-to-move controls or a virtual joystick, interacting with objects and characters by tapping them to gather clues, initiate dialogue, and solve environmental puzzles. Core gameplay involves solving 3-5 puzzles per floor, such as finding specific items, combining inventory objects, or activating mechanisms, which are crucial for unlocking new areas and progressing the linear narrative. Dialogue choices, presented as on-screen buttons, influence character interactions and occasionally impact immediate puzzle solutions, though the overall story progression remains fixed across its multiple episodes. Failure conditions include falling into traps or being caught by enemies, resulting in a game over and requiring a restart from the last auto-save checkpoint, with successful episode completion unlocking exclusive "next episode preview end cards."
name: 殺戮の天使

concept: **Rusty Lake: Roots KR:** Rusty Lake: Roots is a premium point-and-click adventure game where players unravel the Vanderboom family's history across 33+ distinct levels, each representing a pivotal moment in their lineage. Players interact with the environment by tapping on objects, characters, and directional arrows to explore scenes, collect inventory items, and trigger dialogue or events. Core gameplay involves solving interconnected puzzles within each level, often requiring players to drag-and-drop inventory items onto specific environmental elements, combine items, or decipher clues to manipulate objects and progress. Successfully completing a level unlocks the next chronological segment of the family tree, represented by a new character portrait, and advances the overarching narrative, with no traditional "loss" conditions as puzzles can be retried indefinitely. Progression is entirely linear through the 33+ levels, with additional achievements earned by discovering hidden secrets or completing specific optional tasks, further expanding the game's lore.
name: Rusty Lake: Roots KR

concept: **Die drei ??? – Ruf der Trolle:** Players navigate various locations in Rocky Beach by tapping on interactive hotspots to move, examine objects, and initiate dialogue with NPCs. The core gameplay loop involves collecting clues, combining inventory items, and solving narrative-driven logical puzzles, such as deciphering coded messages or reconstructing events from witness testimonies. Progression is linear, with the successful resolution of each puzzle or completion of a dialogue tree unlocking new scenes, character interactions, or advancing the overarching "troll" mystery storyline. Difficulty scales through the increasing complexity of multi-step puzzles and the subtlety of required deductions, while the user interface features a persistent inventory bar and contextual action icons for interactions. The game concludes upon solving the central mystery, with no explicit failure states beyond being stuck on a puzzle, and new locations become accessible only after specific story triggers are met.
name: Die drei ??? – Ruf der Trolle

concept: **Eight-Minute Empire:** Players engage in a turn-based area control strategy game, where each turn they select one of six face-up action cards from a central market. Each selected card grants a specific resource (e.g., 1 coin, 1 food) for end-game scoring and an immediate action, such as recruiting 2 new troops to a city, moving up to 3 troops across adjacent land territories, or sailing 1 troop to a new continent. Control of map regions and continents is determined by troop majority, with actions like 'Destroy Enemy Troops' allowing players to remove opponent units, and 'Build Castle' establishing a fortified presence. The game progresses for a fixed number of rounds, typically 8-10, after which victory points are calculated from collected resources and majority control of regions (e.g., 1-3 points per region) and continents (e.g., 2-5 points per continent). Players interact by tapping on cards to select them, then tapping on map territories to execute troop movements or placements, with the objective being to accumulate the highest total score against 1-4 AI or human opponents.
name: Eight-Minute Empire

concept: **ロックマンX DiVE オフライン (Rockman X DiVE Offline):** Players control iconic "Rockman X" characters in a 2D side-scrolling action platformer, navigating through hundreds of stages within the "Deep Log" cyberworld. Movement is controlled via a virtual joystick on the left side of the screen, while virtual buttons on the right trigger actions such as jumping, dashing, firing a buster, and performing saber attacks, complemented by 360-degree shooting and an auto-lock-on system for simplified combat. Each stage presents a series of platforming challenges and enemy encounters, often culminating in a boss fight, with successful completion unlocking subsequent levels and awarding materials for character and equipment progression. Progression involves collecting and upgrading over 100 unique characters, enhancing them with various weapons, chips, and collectible cards; weapons can be freely combined with any character and ranked up through collected materials to unlock new active or passive skills. Difficulty scales through increasing enemy health, damage output, and spawn rates, alongside more intricate level designs and boss attack patterns, as players strive to repair the corrupted game data and achieve 100% stage completion.
name: ロックマンX DiVE オフライン

concept: **MathLand: Maths pour enfants:** Players control Ray the pirate in a 2D educational adventure game, navigating through over 25 distinct levels across various cursed islands to recover stolen sacred stones. The core gameplay loop involves guiding Ray through platforming segments, avoiding environmental hazards like quicksand and enchanted parrots, and then solving specific math puzzles to unlock progress or retrieve a stone from a chest. Math puzzles are presented as interactive mini-games requiring players to tap the correct answer for operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication tables, or ordering numbers, with difficulty scaling based on selected age groups (e.g., 5-6 year olds focus on 1-10 sums, while 9+ year olds tackle negative numbers and division). Progression is linear through the levels, with new islands becoming accessible after collecting a certain number of sacred stones and potentially acquiring a "spyglass" item, which is earned by completing specific sets of math challenges. The user interface features a virtual joystick for character movement and contextual tap interactions for puzzle solving, with incorrect answers typically resulting in a retry prompt rather than a hard failure state, and the ultimate goal being to complete all math challenges and restore order.
name: MathLand: Maths pour enfants

concept: **Pocket Academy 3:** Players manage and expand a high school on a grid-based map, constructing various facilities like classrooms, cafeterias, and club rooms by tapping on a build menu and dragging them into position. The core loop involves strategically placing compatible facilities adjacent to each other to create "popular spots" that boost student satisfaction and school reputation, which in turn increases student enrollment applications each year. Students progress through grades, join various clubs (e.g., baseball, science), and participate in practice matches and national tournaments, with player management influencing their training and performance through resource allocation and facility upgrades. Progression is driven by increasing school popularity and reputation points, unlocking new and more advanced facilities, clubs, and annual events like school festivals, and guiding students through placement tests to achieve their career dreams. The user interface supports drag-to-scroll navigation and pinch-to-zoom for detailed map management, with success measured by student graduation rates, club victories (e.g., winning 3 national tournaments), and overall school ranking, all while balancing a dynamic budget.
name: Pocket Academy 3

concept: **异常;** "异常" is a single-player puzzle game where players program the AI of robotic units to complete level objectives. In each sequential level, players interact with a visual programming interface, dragging and dropping command blocks or inputting code snippets to define robot behaviors such as "move forward 3 units," "attack nearest enemy," or "wait 2 seconds." After programming, players tap a "Run Simulation" button to observe their robots execute the AI, with success determined by eliminating all enemies or reaching a designated exit point. Progression involves unlocking new levels that introduce additional robot types, environmental hazards, and increasingly complex enemy patterns, requiring players to craft sophisticated AI programs for multiple units to cooperate. Difficulty scales by increasing the number of robots to manage, the intricacy of required AI logic, and the precision needed in command sequencing, with failure occurring if any robot is destroyed or the objective is not met.
name: 异常;
developer: Chengdu Lanfei Huyu Technology Co., Ltd.

concept: **Primee!** "Primee!" is a fast-paced puzzle action game where players identify and interact with falling number balls within a 60-120 second time limit. Numbers continuously spawn from the top of the screen, falling downwards at increasing speeds, and players must tap a number if it is prime, earning points equal to its value. If a falling number is composite, players must swipe across it to "cut" it, which instantly decomposes it into its prime factors (e.g., swiping 6 yields 2 and 3), then quickly tap each resulting prime factor for points. Tapping a composite number directly results in a score penalty (e.g., -5 points), and any number that falls off the bottom of the screen without interaction is lost. The game features 5 difficulty levels, unlocked by achieving specific score milestones, which increase the spawn rate of numbers (e.g., from 1 every 2 seconds to 1 every 0.8 seconds) and introduce larger, more complex numbers to factorize.
name: Primee!

concept: **Everdell** Everdell is a turn-based digital board game adaptation combining worker placement and strategic card play over four distinct seasons. Players take one of three actions per turn: placing a worker on a designated valley space to collect specific resources like 2 berries or 1 twig, playing a Construction or Critter card from their hand into their city tableau (up to 15 cards total) by spending required resources, or preparing for the next season, which recalls all placed workers and advances the game state. Cards played into the city provide ongoing resource generation, unique abilities, and end-game victory points, with players aiming to discover powerful card synergies to maximize their score. The game progresses through Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter, with each season potentially granting additional workers or bonus actions, and the player with the highest total victory points after the fourth season concludes is declared the winner.
name: Everdell

concept: **Dune: Imperium** Dune: Imperium is a turn-based digital board game combining deck-building and worker placement mechanics, played over a series of rounds until a player reaches 10 Victory Points. Each round, players draw 5 cards from their personal deck and then take turns playing cards to deploy their 2 agents to specific locations on the Arrakis board, gaining resources like Spice or Solari, drawing new cards, or advancing on faction influence tracks. After all agents are placed, players reveal remaining cards in hand to gain additional combat strength or other "reveal" effects, then resolve a combat phase where military strength from cards and agents determines control over key territories, awarding Victory Points and bonus resources. Players acquire new, more powerful cards to add to their deck through a market row, continuously refining their strategy to gain influence with 4 factions (Emperor, Bene Gesserit, Spacing Guild, Fremen) and secure the 10 Victory Points required for immediate victory.
name: Dune: Imperium

concept: **Boxville 2** Boxville 2 is a narrative-driven point-and-click puzzle adventure game where players control a red can character, navigating hand-drawn environments to solve a city-wide mystery. Players interact with the world by tapping on objects and characters to trigger animations, collect items into an inventory, or initiate one of dozens of logic puzzles and mini-games. Progression is linear and story-based, requiring players to solve sequential puzzles, such as re-arranging gears to activate a mechanism or decoding visual patterns, to unlock new areas and advance the plot of finding a missing friend and fixing misfired fireworks. The game features no spoken or written dialogue; instead, character intentions and puzzle clues are conveyed through animated thought bubbles and visual sketches, with difficulty scaling through increasingly complex multi-step puzzles and environmental challenges.
name: Boxville 2

concept: **ダンジョンスクワッド (Dungeon Squad):** Dungeon Squad is a roguelike tower defense game where players control a squad of up to 5 monster units to defend against waves of invading heroes across multiple stages. Players strategically tap to deploy monsters into designated slots and activate their unique skills, which have cooldowns ranging from 5 to 30 seconds, to defeat hero units advancing from the right side of the screen. After each wave, players choose one of three random upgrades, such as a new monster, a skill upgrade, or a temporary equipment piece, to enhance their squad's combat effectiveness. Defeated heroes drop "Soul Shards," a meta-currency used between runs to permanently unlock new monster types from a pool of 10 elite monsters and over 100 unique skills, and to upgrade the "Demon World" for global stat bonuses like +5% monster attack or +10% health. A run concludes upon the defeat of all player monsters, with the objective being to clear as many stages as possible and conquer hero bases to progress through the overarching campaign map.
name: ダンジョンスクワッド

concept: **文嘉 (WENJIA):** WENJIA is a 2D side-scrolling platformer where players control a cat-like energy creature, navigating through a series of linear levels to rescue trapped spirits. Players use a virtual joystick on the left side of the screen for horizontal movement and a dedicated jump button on the right to traverse platforms and avoid environmental hazards. The core mechanic involves tapping a "World Shift" button to instantly switch between two parallel dimensions: a vibrant "Material World" and an ethereal "Energy World," each featuring unique platforms, obstacles, and enemy placements. Puzzles require precise timing and world-switching to bypass impassable barriers in one dimension by shifting to the other, or to activate specific mechanisms that only exist in a particular world. Progression is level-based, with each of the approximately 30 levels introducing increasingly complex platforming challenges and multi-dimensional puzzles, and the player fails if they fall into a pit or collide with an enemy.
name: 文嘉

concept: **Strange Horticulture:** Strange Horticulture is an occult puzzle game where players manage a plant shop, primarily interacting with a desk-based UI via taps and drags. Each in-game day, customers arrive with requests for specific plants, providing cryptic clues or symptoms, which players must deduce by cross-referencing a growing inventory of 50+ unique plants with an in-game encyclopedia containing detailed descriptions and illustrations. Players drag and drop the identified plant from their shelves onto the customer's order slot, and incorrect identifications may lead to adverse story consequences or customer dissatisfaction. Further progression involves using textual clues from customers or found notes to click on specific locations on a regional map, revealing new plants or lore, with successful identification unlocking new encyclopedia entries and advancing the overarching narrative mystery. The game's difficulty scales as clues become more abstract and the variety of similar-looking plants increases, requiring more precise observation and deduction to avoid story-altering mistakes.
name: Strange Horticulture

concept: **WINGSPAN (FLÜGELSCHLAG):** Wingspan is a digital adaptation of a competitive engine-building board game for 1-5 players, played over four rounds, where the objective is to attract the most birds to one's nature preserve to score the highest victory points. Players interact via a tap-based interface, selecting one of four actions per turn: playing a bird card from their hand into one of three habitats (Forest, Grassland, Wetland) by paying food and egg costs, gaining food tokens, laying eggs, or drawing new bird cards. Each habitat action becomes more powerful as more birds are played into it, triggering the "When Activated" abilities of all birds in that habitat, creating powerful chain reactions. The number of available actions decreases each round, from 8 in the first to 5 in the final round, forcing strategic optimization of resource generation and bird placement. Points are accumulated from the value of played bird cards, fulfilled bonus cards, and shared end-of-round goals, with the player achieving the highest total score at the end of round four declared the winner.
name: WINGSPAN (FLÜGELSCHLAG)

concept: **Taboo - le jeu de officiel:** Taboo is a digital party game for 2-6 players, where two teams compete to guess the most words within a time limit. During a team's turn, one player acts as the clue-giver, viewing a target word and five associated "taboo" words on their screen, while their teammates attempt to guess the target word. The clue-giver taps "Correct" for successful guesses or "Skip" to pass on a card, but must avoid using any of the taboo words; if a taboo word is spoken, the opposing team taps a "Buzzer" button, resulting in a point deduction for the clue-giver's team. Each round typically lasts 60 seconds, during which the clue-giver aims to describe as many words as possible, and the game progresses through a customizable number of rounds, such as 3-5 rounds. The team with the highest cumulative score at the end of all rounds is declared the winner.
name: Taboo - le jeu de officiel

concept: **WINGSPAN (윙스팬)** Wingspan is a turn-based digital board game for 1-5 players, where each player manages a personal player board with three habitat rows (forest, grassland, wetland). Players use tap controls to select one of four actions per turn: play a bird card, gain food, lay eggs, or draw cards. Choosing an action activates its corresponding habitat, triggering the "when activated" powers of all birds previously placed in that row, from right to left, creating strategic chain combos. Players spend specific food tokens (e.g., 2 worms, 1 fish) to play bird cards, which then contribute points, provide passive benefits, or enable additional actions like laying up to 5 eggs per bird. The game proceeds through a fixed number of rounds, typically four, with points accumulated from played birds, eggs, cached food, and completed bonus cards or end-of-round goals, and the player with the highest total score at the end wins.
name: WINGSPAN (윙스팬)

concept: **Aventureros al Tren®** Ticket to Ride is a turn-based digital board game where players compete to build railway routes across a map. Players begin with a hand of train car cards (e.g., 4 cards) and destination tickets, interacting via tap controls to perform one of three actions per turn: draw two train car cards from a visible row or blind deck, draw new destination tickets (keeping at least one), or claim a route between two cities. To claim a route, players must play a set of matching colored train car cards equal to the route's length (e.g., 1-6 segments), then place their train pieces on that route. The game concludes when a player has three or fewer train pieces remaining, after which points are tallied for claimed routes, completed destination tickets, and the longest continuous railway, with the player achieving the highest score declared the winner.
name: Aventureros al Tren®

concept: **Kingdom Rush 5: Alliance TD** Kingdom Rush 5: Alliance TD is a real-time tower defense game where players defend against waves of 45+ unique enemies across 25 campaign stages. Players strategically place and upgrade 18 distinct tower types (e.g., archers, mages, demon pits) on fixed plots along enemy paths using tap controls, aiming to prevent enemies from reaching the base (typically 20 health points). A key mechanic involves simultaneously controlling two of 16 unlockable heroes, each possessing unique tap-activated abilities and movable along the path to engage enemies directly. Progression involves completing stages to unlock new towers and heroes, which can then be upgraded via skill trees or in-game currency, while difficulty scales through increasing enemy numbers, varied enemy types with specific resistances, and challenging boss encounters.
name: Kingdom Rush 5: Alliance TD

concept: **Isle of Arrows – Tower Defense** Isle of Arrows is a roguelike puzzle tower defense game where players build a fortress on a floating island using randomly generated tiles. Each round, players receive a free tile (e.g., towers, roads, flags, gardens) to place on the grid via tap controls, with the option to spend coins to skip to the next tile. Towers attack invaders, roads extend the enemy path, flags expand the island's buildable area, and gardens generate coins, with over 50 unique tile types offering varied strategic interactions (e.g., taverns boosting adjacent archery towers). Once defenses are set, players tap a "Call Wave" button to unleash enemies, and the run ends if too many invaders escape the island. Progression involves completing campaign stages to unlock new tiles, enemies, and bonus cards (75+ available) for subsequent runs, increasing strategic depth and difficulty.
name: Isle of Arrows – Tower Defense

concept: **The Lost City** The Lost City is a classic point-and-click adventure game where players explore a mysterious, mist-shrouded city to uncover its secrets. Players navigate between static, pre-rendered scenes by tapping on directional arrows or scene transitions, and interact with the environment by tapping on various hotspots to examine objects, collect items, or trigger dialogue. Puzzles are solved by combining collected inventory items (accessed via a bottom-screen UI) with specific environmental elements, or by deciphering clues recorded in an in-game journal. Progression is linear and story-driven, unlocking new areas and advancing the narrative as puzzles are successfully completed, with the ultimate goal being to uncover the city's ancient magic and complete the central quest.
name: The Lost City

concept: **王国保卫战4 (Kingdom Rush Vengeance):** Players strategically deploy and upgrade 22 distinct tower types along fixed paths on a map by tapping empty slots and selecting from a UI menu, aiming to prevent over 60 enemy types from reaching the exit portal. Each tower has 3-4 upgrade tiers, branching into two specialized forms at tier 3, offering unique damage types and abilities, while 17 controllable heroes with 3-4 active abilities gain experience and level up during missions. Waves of enemies, varying in health, armor, and special attacks, spawn at increasing frequencies and speeds across 42 levels, requiring strategic tower placement and hero management. Progression involves earning stars from level completion to unlock new towers, heroes, and 30 permanent upgrades, with difficulty scaling through denser waves, stronger enemies, and 7 unique boss encounters. Players win a level by defeating all enemy waves without letting a set number of enemies (typically 20) pass through the exit portal, or lose if too many enemies escape.
name: 王国保卫战4

concept: **Earn to Die:** Players control one of 8 vehicles in a 2D side-scrolling environment, automatically accelerating through zombie hordes, tapping the right side of the screen for a limited nitro boost and the left side to control vehicle pitch in the air. Each run begins with a full fuel tank and limited nitro, with the objective to cover maximum distance and reach checkpoints in the story mode. Colliding with zombies or terrain reduces vehicle health and momentum, and the run ends if fuel depletes or the vehicle is destroyed, earning cash based on distance and zombies killed. This cash is then spent on multi-tiered upgrades for engine, fuel, wheels, armor, and weapons like guns or spiked frames, directly improving vehicle performance and zombie-killing efficiency. Difficulty scales as zombie density and terrain complexity increase with distance, requiring continuous upgrades to progress through the cross-country journey.
name: Earn to Die

concept: **Little Inferno HD:** Players interact with a virtual fireplace by tapping and dragging various "toys" from an inventory catalog into the fire, observing their unique burning properties, visual effects, and sounds. The core mechanic involves discovering over 50 "combos" by burning specific pairs or groups of items simultaneously, hinted at by cryptic phrases, which rewards the player with stamps and unlocks new catalog entries. Progression is driven by discovering these combos and burning items to earn coins, which are then used to purchase new items from the catalog, with new catalogs unlocking sequentially as story events trigger. The game features no traditional win/loss states; instead, it focuses on a narrative journey where players receive letters and progress through a linear story by continuously burning items and discovering all available combos.
name: Little Inferno HD

concept: **7 Days to End with You:** Players are presented with a character speaking an unknown language within a confined environment, where the core mechanic involves deducing word meanings. By tapping on interactive objects in the room, players reveal single words in the unknown language, which they then associate with context and manually input a guessed translation into a persistent personal dictionary via a dedicated UI. The game progresses over a fixed 7-day in-game period, with time advancing after specific interactions, and the narrative branches significantly based on the player's interpreted word meanings, influencing character reactions and story outcomes. There are no traditional win/loss conditions; instead, the objective is to gradually understand the language to uncover one of multiple narrative endings within the 7-day cycle. The game encourages multiple playthroughs, allowing players to reset their interpretations and explore different story paths and conclusions.
name: 7 Days to End with You

concept: **ウボンゴ – ダス・ワイルド・レゲスピエル (Ubongo):** Players are presented with a grid-based puzzle board and 3-4 unique polyomino pieces, with the core mechanic being to drag and drop these pieces using touch to perfectly fill the designated area without overlap or gaps. Tapping a piece rotates it 90 degrees clockwise, while a two-finger pinch gesture flips it, allowing precise manipulation. In "Level Mode," players progress through hundreds of increasingly difficult puzzles, earning in-game "gems" based on completion speed, which can unlock hints or new puzzle sets. "Free Mode" offers over 6,000 randomly generated puzzles, and "Time Attack" challenges players to solve as many as possible within a 60-second limit. Multiplayer mode allows up to 4 players to simultaneously solve individual puzzles, with the fastest correct solver earning the most gems.
name: ウボンゴ – ダス・ワイルド・レゲスピエル

concept: **ダンジョン・ウォーフェア2 (Dungeon Warfare 2):** Dungeon Warfare 2 is a top-down tower defense game where players strategically place 33 unique traps on a grid-based dungeon path to eliminate waves of 30+ enemy types. Players tap on empty grid cells to select and deploy traps from a UI menu, each possessing 8 upgradeable traits like increased damage or area-of-effect. Successful defense earns gold for purchasing new traps and upgrading existing ones, alongside experience points (XP) that unlock skill points for three distinct skill trees, each containing 11 passive abilities. Progression involves completing 60+ developer-designed levels and procedurally generated dungeons, with difficulty scaling via combinable runes that increase enemy stats (e.g., +20% HP, +15% speed) for greater rewards. The core loop involves defending against waves, upgrading traps, and developing skill trees, with a level ending in victory if fewer than 20 enemies escape, or defeat if the escape limit is reached.
name: ダンジョン・ウォーフェア2

concept: **レッドホーン家の探偵 (Redhorn Family Detective):** Redhorn Family Detective is a narrative-driven point-and-click adventure game where players guide Edward, a young detective, through a small town to solve a serial murder case. Players interact with the environment by tapping on objects to examine them, collecting clues, and initiating dialogue with suspects and witnesses from a dedicated UI menu. Progression unfolds linearly across multiple chapters, requiring players to combine collected evidence in their inventory and make logical deductions to unlock new locations, advance the story, and uncover Redhorn family secrets. The difficulty scales with the complexity of the deduction puzzles, which might involve connecting 3-5 disparate clues to form a coherent theory. The primary objective is to successfully solve the overarching murder mystery, leading to a conclusive narrative ending, with potential for multiple endings based on key deductions.
name: 레드호른가의 탐정

concept: **ノベルズローグ ～異世界古書堂と封印の魔女～ (Novels Rogue ~Otherworld Old Bookstore and the Sealed Witch~):** Novels Rogue is a roguelike deck-building card game where players delve into procedurally generated dungeons, with each run resetting their deck. Players engage in turn-based combat, tapping to select cards from their hand and targeting enemies, with victory allowing them to choose one new card from three options to build their current run's deck. The game features four distinct combat systems, determined by the chosen "book": "Total Attack" where all characters use a single card, "Hamiel Counter" utilizing a built gauge for powerful actions, "Devisoul" for mana substitution, and "Sorcery" allowing up to three unique, once-per-combat spells. Progression involves completing dungeon runs across different "books" to unlock new cards for the global pool, increase heroine affection by gifting treasures, and fill a card encyclopedia, with an optional difficulty increase system for enhanced challenge and rewards. A run concludes upon clearing all dungeon floors and defeating the boss, or upon all player characters' HP reaching zero, necessitating a fresh start.
name: ノベルズローグ ～異世界古書堂と封印の魔女～

concept: **Glow Puzzle:** Glow Puzzle is a single-player line-drawing puzzle game where players must connect all given lines on a grid of dots without retracing any segment, forming a continuous Eulerian path. Players can choose between two control schemes: tapping a starting dot and then sequentially tapping adjacent dots to extend the line, or tapping and dragging a finger between adjacent dots to draw connections. The game features over 950 pre-designed puzzles, with progression occurring linearly as players successfully complete each puzzle to unlock the next. Difficulty scales by increasing the grid size, the number of dots, and the complexity of the required path, demanding more intricate logical deduction. The core loop involves solving individual puzzles, with success marked by completing the required path, and failure simply allowing a reset to attempt the puzzle again.
name: Glow Puzzle

concept: **Very Little Nightmares:** Very Little Nightmares is an isometric 2.5D puzzle adventure game where players guide the Girl in the Yellow Raincoat through a hostile mansion called "The Nest." Players control the girl by tapping on the screen to direct her movement to specific locations and tapping on interactive objects like levers, crates, or climbable surfaces to manipulate the environment. Progression is linear, with each room presenting environmental puzzles that require players to observe surroundings, utilize physics-based interactions (e.g., pushing a block onto a pressure plate), or find specific items to unlock new pathways. Stealth mechanics are crucial, demanding players to time movements and hide from enemies with defined patrol routes and detection cones, as being caught or falling into one of the numerous traps (e.g., spikes, bottomless pits) results in an immediate restart from the last checkpoint. The core objective is to solve all puzzles and evade all threats to escape the mansion and advance the overarching narrative.
name: Very Little Nightmares

concept: **艾彼 - 大结局更新 (Abi)** Players control the robot Abi, and sometimes her companion DD, through a post-apocalyptic world filled with environmental puzzles and narrative exploration. Movement is achieved by tapping on the desired location or using a virtual joystick, while interacting with objects or other robots is done via contextual tap prompts. Puzzles often require switching between Abi and DD, utilizing Abi's smaller size to access tight spaces or DD's industrial strength to move heavy objects, with solutions typically involving 2-3 steps of object manipulation or sequence activation. Progression is linear, advancing the story through distinct chapters as players uncover clues about humanity's disappearance and unlock new areas after solving key puzzles. The game concludes upon discovering the final truth in the metropolis, with no explicit failure states beyond being stuck on a puzzle.
name: 艾彼 - 大结局更新

concept: **Meridian 157: Kapitel 1** Players navigate a mysterious subarctic facility as David Zander in a first-person point-and-click adventure. Movement between static scenes is achieved by tapping directional arrows displayed on screen, while interacting with environmental objects or collecting items involves tapping specific hotspots. Puzzles range from inventory-based combinations, where collected items are dragged from a bottom-screen inventory onto relevant scene elements, to logic puzzles requiring players to decipher codes or manipulate mechanisms with 3-5 distinct steps. Progression is linear, with each solved puzzle unlocking new areas or advancing the narrative, and a contextual hint system is available after a 60-second cooldown for each progressive clue. The game concludes upon solving the overarching mystery of Meridian 157, with no failure conditions beyond being unable to solve a puzzle.
name: Meridian 157: Kapitel 1

concept: **서울 2033 : 후원자 (Seoul 2033: Supporter)** Seoul 2033 is a text-based roguelike where players navigate a post-apocalyptic Seoul by making choices from presented narrative prompts. Each decision, made by tapping on one of 2-4 on-screen options, directly impacts the player's core stats such as Health, Stress, Money, and specific abilities like Strength or Intelligence, which are displayed in a persistent UI. The core loop involves managing these resources through a series of randomly encountered events, where poor choices can lead to negative stat effects or resource consumption, while good choices yield rewards or open new story paths. Progression is achieved through completing specific story arcs, unlocking over 350 unique narrative events across multiple runs, and earning achievements that persist between playthroughs. A run concludes when Health drops to 0 or Stress reaches its maximum of 100, or upon reaching a specific narrative ending, with the objective being to survive and uncover as many story branches as possible.
name: 서울 2033 : 후원자

concept: **샐리의 법칙 (Sally's Law)** Sally's Law is a unique 2D puzzle platformer featuring a two-phase gameplay loop for each level. First, players control Sally, who automatically runs forward and can jump by tapping the screen, experiencing seemingly miraculous "luck" as obstacles vanish and paths open just in time. Immediately after, players replay the exact same level as Sally's father, a spirit who can manipulate the environment by tapping or dragging objects like platforms and switches, with the objective to actively create the "luck" Sally experienced by clearing her path within precise 1-2 second timing windows. Progression is linear, with each completed two-phase level unlocking the next, and the narrative unfolds through inter-level cutscenes revealing the father-daughter story. Failure occurs if Sally falls into a hazard or gets stuck, or if the father fails to clear her path, requiring a full level restart.
name: 샐리의 법칙

concept: **Duck Life 7: Battle** Duck Life 7: Battle is a duck training and combat RPG where players customize a duck and enhance its five core stats: Power, Health, Defence, Speed, and Special Attack. Players explore 6 distinct world areas using tap-to-move controls, discovering 5 training dojos, each containing 5 unique mini-games with specific tap or swipe controls to improve individual stats, yielding 1-5 stat points per successful completion. The core loop involves training to increase stats, then engaging in over 40 turn-based battles against other ducks, where victory is achieved by reducing the opponent's 100 Health Points to zero through attacks determined by Power and Speed stats. Progression involves winning tournaments, completing quests for rewards, and unlocking 100 different weapons and costumes that provide stat bonuses (e.g., +10 Power, +5 Defence), ultimately aiming to become the world's greatest duck battler.
name: Duck Life 7: Battle

concept: **Guns'n'Glory Premium:** Players deploy and reposition mobile bandit units along predefined paths to ambush waves of pioneer enemies like settlers and stagecoaches, preventing them from escaping the map. Units automatically attack enemies within their specific range (e.g., 50 pixels), dealing damage based on their type, while players can tap a unit and then a new location to manually move it or pursue escaping targets. Defeating enemies awards gold, which is spent in a UI menu to recruit new unit types (e.g., Crazy Mexicans, Brave Indians) or upgrade existing units' damage output by 15% or range by 10 pixels. Progression involves completing levels across distinct regions like Alaska and Nevada, where enemy waves increase in number and health (e.g., settlers 10 HP, stagecoaches 50 HP), and failure occurs if more than 10 enemies reach the map exit. Power-ups, such as temporary attack boosts, can be collected from defeated special enemies or as level rewards, adding tactical depth to the core loop of placement, upgrading, and defense.
name: Guns'n'Glory Premium

concept: **Super Stickman Golf 2:** Players control a stickman golfer, dragging a finger on the screen to adjust shot angle and power, then releasing to hit the golf ball across 2D physics-based courses. The objective is to sink the ball into the hole in the fewest strokes possible, navigating dynamic obstacles like moving platforms, portals, and magnets. Each shot consumes one stroke, and players can activate limited-use power-ups (e.g., 3 sticky balls per round) before a shot to alter ball physics. Progression involves unlocking 32 new courses by completing previous ones, and earning "Golf Bux" from low stroke counts or collecting "Golf Balls" on courses. This currency unlocks 14 new golfer skins and 34 hats, with each hat providing a unique passive ability (e.g., increased shot accuracy or bounce). Multiplayer modes include real-time "Race Mode" for up to 3 online players to reach the hole first, and asynchronous "Turn Based Multiplayer" where players take turns on a course.
name: Super Stickman Golf 2

concept: **A Dark Room:** Players interact with a minimalist text-based interface, tapping on highlighted words or buttons to perform actions and manage resources. The core loop begins with "light fire" and "collect wood," with wood consumed to maintain the fire, which in turn attracts "wanderers" and unlocks new building options. Building structures like "huts" (e.g., costing 100 wood) expands the village, automating resource generation (e.g., "wood +1/second") and unlocking new actions such as "hunting" or "trapping." Progression is driven by narrative discovery and resource accumulation, eventually leading to the ability to embark on "expeditions" to explore a map, where players manage health points (e.g., 100 HP) during turn-based combat encounters and gather rare materials. The game concludes upon reaching a specific narrative endpoint, with no explicit loss condition in the early stages beyond resource depletion halting progress.
name: A Dark Room

concept: **Bring You Home:** Players do not control the protagonist Polo directly; instead, they manipulate the environment by dragging and dropping or tapping to rearrange pre-defined level segments (panels) on the screen. Polo automatically moves along the path created by these panels, and the objective is to arrange them to guide him safely past obstacles like spikes or enemies to the level's exit. Each level presents a unique set of panels containing various hazards and helpful elements, requiring players to strategically sequence them to form a continuous, safe route. Progression occurs by completing all levels within a "world" to unlock the next, with difficulty scaling through increased panel complexity and the introduction of new environmental mechanics. If Polo encounters a deadly obstacle, the player can tap a "rewind" button to undo his movement and attempt a different panel arrangement, with success defined by reaching the exit and failure by any fatal interaction.
name: Bring You Home

concept: **Skul: The Hero Slayer:** Players control Skul, a tiny skeleton, through procedurally generated 2D platformer levels, using a virtual joystick for movement and virtual buttons for basic attacks, special abilities, and dashing. The core mechanic involves collecting and swapping between over 100 unique "skulls," each granting Skul a distinct moveset (e.g., melee, ranged) and a powerful special ability with a cooldown (e.g., 10 seconds). Defeated enemies drop "Dark Quartz" and random "items" that provide passive stat buffs like +15 attack or +5% critical chance, creating synergistic builds. Progression is run-based; players clear rooms and defeat bosses across multiple biomes, with death (HP reaching 0) resulting in the loss of all current run items and skulls. However, accumulated "Dark Quartz" can be spent at a central hub between runs to unlock permanent upgrades, such as increased starting gold or new item pool additions, making subsequent runs incrementally easier.
name: Skul: The Hero Slayer

concept: **Shadow Of Death: Premium Games:** Players control a chosen hero in a 2D side-scrolling action RPG, navigating linear stages using a virtual joystick for movement and on-screen buttons to execute basic attacks and 3-5 unique active skills, each with specific mana costs and cooldowns (e.g., 5-15 seconds). Combat involves engaging waves of diverse enemies, requiring strategic use of skills to deplete their health points, with successful hits generating mana and experience points. Progression is tied to hero leveling, which unlocks new skill tree nodes that enhance abilities (e.g., +10% damage, -0.5s cooldown) and allows equipping various rarity gear (common to legendary) dropped by enemies, modifying stats like attack, defense, and health. Stages feature increasing enemy density, mini-bosses, and final boss encounters with unique attack patterns, while an "Arena" mode pits players against AI-controlled versions of other heroes in 3-round matches for exclusive gear. A stage is won by defeating all enemy waves and the final boss, but the player loses if their hero's health points drop to zero, requiring a retry.
name: Shadow Of Death: Premium Games

concept: **雨纪 (Rainy Day):** Players control a mysterious girl in a 2D isometric puzzle environment, navigating through flooded city sections by tapping on the screen to move her to specific interactive points. The core mechanic involves tapping on various light sources, such as lanterns and ancient steles, to activate them, which causes localized water levels to recede, revealing new pathways or activating other environmental mechanisms like movable platforms. Across 9 chapters and over 60 levels, new puzzle elements are progressively introduced, including light-sensitive switches, reflective crystals, and pressure plates, requiring players to chain light activations and manipulate multiple elements to clear obstacles. Successfully completing a level unlocks the next, while optional challenges within each level include collecting hidden "cores" (typically 3 per level) and activating all dormant robots, contributing to a chapter's S-rank rating if completed with minimal moves. A level is won when the girl reaches the designated exit point, having manipulated the environment to clear her path, with no explicit loss state other than failing to solve the puzzle.
name: 雨纪

concept: **Die drei ??? – Chamäleonbande:** Players engage in a narrative-driven detective adventure, primarily interacting with the environment and characters by tapping on hotspots to examine objects, collect clues, and initiate dialogue. The game progresses through a series of distinct locations (e.g., 5-7 per chapter), where players must gather specific clues (e.g., 3-5 pieces of evidence per scene) and interview suspects by selecting dialogue options from a branching tree, with certain choices unlocking new information or paths. Interspersed throughout the investigation are mini-puzzles, such as deciphering codes, assembling torn documents, or matching fingerprints, which require logical deduction or pattern recognition to solve and unlock further story segments. The main objective is to piece together the mystery of the "Chameleon Gang" by accumulating enough evidence and making correct deductions, which are tracked in a case file UI, leading to the identification and apprehension of the culprits. The game concludes successfully when all key clues are found, all suspects are interrogated, and the player correctly identifies the Chameleon Gang members and their motives, leading to the case's resolution, with no explicit failure state other than being unable to progress without solving a puzzle.
name: Die drei ??? – Chamäleonbande

concept: **Layton : l'étrange village HD:** Players control Professor Layton and his apprentice Luke in a point-and-click adventure, navigating static 2D scenes by tapping directional arrows to move between locations and tapping on objects or characters to interact. The primary mechanic involves discovering over 100 logic puzzles by tapping on suspicious environmental hotspots or engaging in dialogue with the village's 30+ eccentric inhabitants, each puzzle presenting a unique challenge like sliding blocks, matchstick rearrangements, or riddles. Upon encountering a puzzle, players are presented with a dedicated interface where they input their solution (e.g., dragging pieces, typing numbers); if stuck, "Hint Coins" (found by tapping random spots in the environment, up to 10 per scene) can be spent to reveal up to 3 progressive hints. Solving puzzles advances the main narrative, unlocking new areas of the village and story segments, while optional mini-games involve collecting "trinkets" from solved puzzles to assemble a robot dog, or furniture to decorate a room, offering additional challenges. The game is won by successfully solving all mandatory puzzles and uncovering the mystery of the Golden Apple, with no explicit loss condition, though failing to solve a puzzle prevents story progression until it is either solved or skipped using a sufficient number of Hint Coins.
name: Layton : l'étrange village HD

concept: **Kingdom Rush Origins - TD:** Players defend a set number of entry points from waves of over 30 distinct enemy types by strategically placing and upgrading 4 core tower types (Archer, Mage, Barracks, Stone Druid) along predefined paths on a 2D map. Each core tower can be upgraded up to 3 times, then specialized into one of two advanced forms (e.g., Elf Archers to Golden Longbows or Wild Magus), each possessing 3 unique active or passive abilities that can be further upgraded using collected stars. Defeating enemies grants gold, which is used to build new towers or upgrade existing ones, while a chosen hero (from 16 available, each with 3 unique skills and 1 ultimate ability) can be moved freely along the path and spells like "Reinforcements" or "Lightning Bolt" can be cast with cooldowns (e.g., 20-60 seconds). Completing levels earns up to 3 stars based on remaining lives (starting with 20), which are spent on a global upgrade tree to enhance tower stats (e.g., +5% damage, -10% cost) and hero abilities, with 3 difficulty settings (Casual, Normal, Veteran) and 3 challenge modes (Classic, Heroic, Iron) offering varied enemy compositions and restrictions. A level is won by successfully eliminating all enemy waves before 20 enemies breach the final exit point, while losing all lives results in mission failure, requiring a restart of the level.
name: Kingdom Rush Origins - TD

concept: **森丘露营地 (Forest Hill Campsite)** Players manage a pixel-art camping ground, using a tap-and-place interface to strategically position various facilities like fishing spots, campfire BBQ areas, and tent pitches across a multi-biome map. The core loop involves attracting AI-controlled pixel campers by satisfying their "wishes" for activities like bug catching or fishing, which are fulfilled by having the corresponding facilities available. Players also manage a camping goods shop, developing and stocking new items from a catalog of over 50 unique products, with sales generating in-game currency. Progression involves using this currency to unlock new facility blueprints and expand the campsite's area, while difficulty scales as campers demand a wider variety of activities and higher satisfaction ratings, requiring optimal layout and resource management. The game features an idle observation mode where the campsite operates autonomously through day/night and seasonal cycles, with the primary goal being to achieve a 5-star rating and attract a maximum capacity of 100 campers.
name: 森丘露营地
developer: Xiamen Leiting Network Technology Co., Ltd.

concept: **Solo tú por aquí (Only you here)** Players navigate a first-person psychological horror adventure set within a dreamscape, using a virtual joystick on the left side of the screen for movement and swiping the right side to control camera rotation. The core mechanic involves exploring surreal environments, interacting with environmental objects via a single-tap action to uncover clues, solve contextual puzzles, and progress towards opening a specific "small door" to escape. Puzzles range from finding hidden keys to manipulating machinery, with each successful interaction revealing a piece of the fragmented narrative. Progression is driven by unlocking new areas after solving 1-3 interconnected puzzles per zone, leading to one of approximately 7 distinct narrative endings based on player choices and discovered secrets. The game concludes upon reaching any of the multiple endings, with the ultimate goal being to uncover the "main secret" by experiencing all possible narrative paths.
name: Solo tú por aquí

concept: **存在/しないあなた、と私 (You and I, Existing/Non-existing)** This is a short psychological horror visual novel where players primarily interact by tapping the screen to advance dialogue and narrative text. The core mechanic revolves around making critical choices presented as on-screen buttons, with over 100 distinct decision points influencing the story's progression and the protagonist's relationship with a mysterious girl. Each choice branches the narrative, leading to different dialogue sequences, character reactions, and ultimately one of approximately 12 unique endings that explore themes of existence and reality. Progression is achieved by unlocking new narrative paths through repeated playthroughs and selecting different choices, with the goal being to uncover the "world's truth" by experiencing all possible outcomes. The game features no explicit win or loss conditions beyond reaching a narrative conclusion, encouraging players to explore every branch of the story tree.
name: 存在/しないあなた、と私

concept: **The House of Da Vinci** Players explore a 3D Renaissance workshop in first-person, using swipe gestures to look around and pinch-to-zoom for closer inspection of intricate mechanical puzzles. The core gameplay involves interacting with detailed 3D objects via tap-and-drag or rotate gestures to manipulate gears, levers, and hidden compartments, solving complex puzzles inspired by Leonardo Da Vinci's inventions. A unique "Oculus Perpetua" gauntlet, activated by a dedicated UI button, allows players to see through surfaces to reveal hidden mechanisms or rewind specific environmental events to uncover clues. Progression is linear, with each room functioning as an escape puzzle where solving 3-5 interconnected mechanical challenges unlocks access to the next area and advances the overarching narrative of the master's disappearance. The game concludes upon solving the final grand puzzle and uncovering the truth, with no explicit loss conditions beyond failing to solve a puzzle.
name: The House of Da Vinci

concept: **Convenience Stories** Players manage a convenience store using a grid-based tap-and-place interface to arrange shelves, product displays, and specialty sections like a newsstand or hot food counter. The core loop involves stocking a diverse inventory of over 150 unique products, from candy to hot meals, to satisfy varying customer demands and generate revenue. Players recruit and assign up to 10 employees, each with different stats, to tasks like stocking shelves or operating the register, ensuring 24-hour service. Progression is driven by accumulating profits to unlock new product types via supplier negotiations, expand the store's physical footprint by purchasing adjacent grid tiles, and upgrade facilities to increase customer satisfaction and store appeal. The game features a competitive element where players must outperform rival stores in sales and customer ratings to "crush" them and expand their franchise territory, with the ultimate goal being to achieve the highest market share and a 5-star store rating.
name: Convenience Stories

concept: **DEEMO**: Players interact with a vertical scrolling interface where musical notes descend from the top of the screen towards a horizontal judgment line at the bottom. Tapping a single note as it aligns with the judgment line registers a hit, while holding and releasing long notes at the correct timing, or swiping horizontally across multiple connected notes, executes specific musical phrases. Successful and accurate note execution contributes to a score and a combo multiplier, with performance graded from "Charming" to "Miss," and completing songs contributes to the growth of a central tree in the story mode, adding 0.5m to 2.0m to its height per play. Reaching specific tree heights (e.g., 5m, 10m, 20m) unlocks new story scenes, additional playable songs from a library of over 220 tracks, and new explorable areas containing hidden elements. Each song offers multiple difficulty levels (Easy, Normal, Hard), increasing the density and complexity of note patterns, requiring faster reflexes and more precise timing, with the primary objective being to achieve the highest possible score and maintain a continuous combo for optimal tree growth and story progression.
name: DEEMO

concept: **Kitty Powers' Matchmaker**: Players manage a virtual matchmaking agency, starting by selecting a client from a queue and then choosing a suitable date from Kitty's Black Book based on matching personality traits and physical preferences displayed on their profiles. Once a match is made, players send the couple on a date to one of several unlockable restaurant venues, each offering different ambiance and a series of context-sensitive mini-games. During dates, players engage in mini-games such as tapping to select appropriate dialogue responses, swiping to apply makeup, or dragging and dropping gifts, all aimed at increasing the couple's "Love Meter" (a percentage bar). Each mini-game has a time limit (e.g., 10-15 seconds), with successful completion awarding 5-10% Love Meter progress, while failures or incorrect choices can reduce it by 5-15%. Successfully completing dates and achieving a high Love Meter percentage (e.g., 75% or more) earns agency reputation points and in-game currency, which unlock new date venues (e.g., "The Greasy Spoon" at 100 points) and agency upgrades like the salon. Difficulty scales as clients present more complex and conflicting preferences, requiring more strategic matching and higher success rates in mini-games, with a date considered successful if the Love Meter reaches a minimum threshold (e.g., 60%) by the end, otherwise it results in negative client reviews.
name: Kitty Powers' Matchmaker
